content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 28, 2015

Brian Pieriek
Burke, Williams & Sorenson, LLP
2310 East Ponderosa Drive – Suite 25
Camarillo, CA 93010-4747

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-15-081

Dear Mr. Pieriek:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Atascadero City Councilmember
Heather Moreno regarding the conflict of interest disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Government Code section 1090.

QUESTION

Is Councilmember Moreno required to include her income from Paranetics on the Form 700
which she files as a City Council Member for the City of Atascadero or on the Form 700 which she
files as an alternate member of the Board of the San Luis Obispo Council of Governments?

CONCLUSION

1 and 2. Councilmember Moreno is required to include her income from Paranetics on the
Form 700 which she files as an alternate member of the Board of the San Luis Obispo Council of
Governments, but she is not required to report the source on the City of Atascadero Form 700.

FACTS

Councilmember Moreno is an Atascadero City Councilmember and in her private capacity
is employed by a company known as Paranetics Technology, Inc. ("Paranetics"). As a City
Councilmember for the City of Atascadero, Councilmember Moreno is required to fill out a Form
700.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-081
Page No. 2

In 2015, Councilmember Moreno will also serve as an alternate for the Board of the San
Luis Obispo Council of Governments ("SLOCOG") which has jurisdiction throughout the County
of San Luis Obispo. As such, the Councilmember will have to complete a Form 700 for her position
with SLOCOG as well.

Paranetics is incorporated in the State of California. Since 2008, Councilmember Moreno
has worked for Paranetics on a part-time basis (1099, not W-2) as controller for Paranetics.
Councilmember Moreno performs accounting services for Paranetics such as payroll, accounts
receivable and accounts payable from her home office in Atascadero. The work is performed via
email/phone/internet. In 2014, Councilmember Moreno received a 1099 form for nonemployee
compensation of $25,357.08 issued by Paranetics listing their San Luis, Arizona address.

Paranetics is a registered corporation with their Cayucos address listed as the "corporate
office." In addition, two of the Paranetics owners live in Cayucos which is in SLO County. You
stated that other than entering into discussions about the business by email or phone, the owners
conduct no actual company business at their home in Cayucos.

Paranetics operations all take place in San Luis, Arizona. The product manufactured by
Paranetics at the Arizona plant is cargo parachutes which are sold to the United States military. As
the United States has pulled out of Afghanistan the need for Paranetics cargo parachutes has halted
and Paranetics is currently "hibernating" with no operations as of January 31, 2015. You stated that
Councilmember Moreno may still perform some services for Paranetics in 2015, but those services
will be very limited.

There has never been an item that has come before the City of Atascadero or SLOCOG
involving Paranetics and it does not appear there would ever be such contact.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in
making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest." (Section
87100.) In addition, certain state and local public officials must file periodic Statements of
Economic Interests (Form 700) disclosing those personal assets and interests that may be affected
during the performance of their official duties. (Sections 87200 - 87350.) Public officials who are
required to file statements of economic interests are either identified in Section 87200 (statutory
filers) or designated in an agency's conflict-of-interest code (designated employees or code filers).²
(See Sections 87300 and 87302.)

² Designated employees who are required to file statements of economic interests under any other agency's
conflict of interest code, or under article 2 for a different jurisdiction, may expand their statement of economic interests
to cover reportable interests in both jurisdictions, and file copies of this expanded statement with both entities in lieu of
filing separate and distinct statements, provided that each copy of such expanded statement filed in place of an original
is signed and verified by the designated employee as if it were an original. You should contact the Commission's
Technical Assistance Divisions for help regarding an expanded statement.

File No. A-15-081
Page No. 3

Officials are required to report sources of income that are located in or doing business in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency. Section 82030 provides that "[i]ncome, other than a gift, does
not include income received from any source outside the jurisdiction and not doing business within
the jurisdiction, not planning to do business within the jurisdiction, or not having done business
within the jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time any statement or other action is
required under this title." (Section 82030(a).)

Regulation 18230 further clarifies that a business is doing business in the jurisdiction if,
among other criteria, it "maintains a physical presence in the jurisdiction . . . ." The Commission has
consistently advised that having an office of any kind in the jurisdiction is sufficient to make the
business a disclosable source of income. For example, in the Sato Advice Letter, No. I-90-325, we
advised:

"Thus, if either the Stockton partnership or the San Jose partnership which
is otherwise related to the Stockton partnership does business in San Jose, the
councilmember's interest in the Stockton partnership would be disclosable. You
stated that the administrative activities of the Stockton partnership are handled at
the San Jose Offices of the San Jose partnership. We believe that this link alone
would be sufficient to meet the 'doing business' requirement of the statute. While
such activity might not constitute 'doing business' in the jurisdiction under other
provisions of law, we believe that these other sections are not determinative
because the other standards were enacted for purposes different than the purposes
of personal financial disclosure under the Act. (In re Baty (1979) 5 FPPC Ops.
10.)"

You stated that two of the Paranetics owners live in Cayucos which is in SLO County and
Paranetics is a registered corporation with their Cayucos address listed as the "corporate office."
You also stated that the owners conduct some company business at their home in Cayucos by email
and phone. Holding the Cayucos location out as a corporate office of the company conducting even
minimal business in Cayucos is sufficient to meet the "doing business" requirement of the statute.³
Consequently, we conclude that the source is disclosable on the Form 700 filed for the SLOCOG⁴
position, but not for the Form 700 filed for the City of Atascadero.

³ Note that part of the rationale for a broad interpretation of the concept under the Act is that a public official
has a potential conflict of interest generally with respect to both the business and the businesses owners. Moreover, under such
circumstances, the disqualification requirement goes beyond decisions that affect the business to decisions that may
affect the owners in their individual capacity.

⁴ The SLOCOG conflict of interest code requires disclosure of investments in, and income from, any business
entity doing business within the County.

File No. A-15-081
Page No. 4


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

sections.question:

which she files as a City Council Member for the City of Atascadero or on the Form 700 which she 
files as an alternate member of the Board of the San Luis Obispo Council of Governments?

1 and 2. Councilmember Moreno is required to include her income from Paranetics on the 
Fonn 700 which she files as an alternate member of the Board of the San Luis Obispo Council of 
Governments, but she is not required to report the source on the City of Atascadero Form 700.

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in 
making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest." (Section 
87100.) In addition, certain state and local public officials must file periodic Statements of

required to file statements of economic interests are either identified in Section 87200 (statutory 
filers) or designated in an agency's conflict-of-interest code (designated employees or code filers).2 
(See Sections 87300 and 87302.) 
2 Designated employees who are required to file statements of economic interests under any other agency's 
conflict of interest code, or under article 2 for a different jurisdiction, may expand their statement of economic interests 
to cover reponable interests in both jurisdictions, and file copies of this expanded statement with both entities in lieu of 
filing separate and distinct statements, provided that each copy of such expanded statement filed in place of an original 
is signed and verified by the designated employee as if it were an original. You should contact the Commission's 
Technical Assistance Divisions for help regarding an expanded statement.  
Officials are required to report sources of income that are located in or doing business in the 
jurisdiction of the official's agency. Section 82030 provides that "[i]ncome, other than a gift, does

the jurisdiction, not planning to do business within the jurisdiction, or not having done business

required under this title." (Section 82030(a).) 
Regulation 18230 further clarifies that a business is doing business in the jurisdiction if, 
among other criteria, it "maintains a physical presence in the jurisdiction .... " The Commission has 
consistently advised that having an office of any kind in the jurisdiction is sufficient to make the 
business a disclosable source of income. For example, in the Sato Advice Letter, No. 1-90-325, we 
advised: 
"Thus, if either the Stockton partnership or the San Jose partnership which 
is otherwise related to the Stockton partnership does business in San Jose, the 
councilmember's interest in the Stockton partnership would be disclosable. You 
stated that the administrative activities of the Stockton partnership are handled at 
the San Jose Offices of the San Jose partnership. We believe that this link alone

such activity might not constitute 'doing business' in the jurisdiction under other

because the other standards were enacted for purposes different than the purposes

10.)"

You also stated that the owners conduct some company business at their home in Cayucos by email 
and phone. Holding the Cayucos location out as a corporate office of the company conducting even 
minimal business in Cayucos is sufficient to meet the "doing business" requirement of the statute.3 
Consequently, we conclude that the source is disclosable on the Form 700 filed for the SLOCOG4 
position, but not for the Form 700 filed for the City of Atascadero. 
3 Note that part of the rationale for a broad interpretation of the concept under the Act is that a public official 
has a conflict of interest generally with respect to both the business and the businesses owners. Moreover, under such 
circumstances, the disqualification requirement goes beyond decisions that affect the business to decisions that may 
affect the owners in their individual capacity. 
4 The SLOCOG conflict of interest code requires disclosure of investments in, and income from, any business 
entity doing business within the County.
